summary: Markets are exhibiting a broadly risk-on mood Friday, with Wall Street indexes jumping sharply after President Trump announced the cancellation of strikes against Iran, easing immediate fears of a broader Middle East conflict. The dominant macro driver is the evolving U.S.-Iran diplomatic situation, where reports of an imminent peace deal have pushed oil prices near two-month lows, boosted European equities, and triggered a rally in the Indian rupee as dollar long positions were unwound. Energy and defense-linked sectors face the most direct repricing pressure from falling oil, while technology remains in focus as a former Tesla board member warned that SpaceX must achieve two of its three major strategic milestones to justify its lofty private valuation, and Honeywell's CEO highlighted AI-driven automation as a key industrial growth theme. The primary risk factors investors are monitoring include conflicting signals from Tehran — with Iran stating no final decision on a deal has been made — alongside unresolved questions about the terms of any agreement, military risks around sites like Kharg Island, and early economic data showing the UK already absorbing fallout from the Iran conflict in April. In the short term, markets are likely to remain highly sensitive to any headline shifts in the U.S.-Iran negotiation, with a confirmed ceasefire or deal capable of sustaining the equity rally, while any breakdown in talks could rapidly reverse oil and risk-asset gains.
